Tafseer: كهيعص۔ These are among the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at). A detailed discussion on these letters can be found at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah.

ذِكْرُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُ زَكَرِيَّا۔ 

**The Special Status of Prophet Zachariah:** The word "dhikr" (remembrance/mention) here carries the same meaning as the imperative form "udhkur" (and mention) used later for all the prophets from Maryam to Idris. This is a directive to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that while the ignorant have forgotten these historical accounts, they are being narrated to you so that you may benefit from them and remind others so they too may benefit. The use of the word "'Abduhu" (His servant) in the verse is evidence of Zachariah's special designation. For someone whom Allah Himself refers to as His servant, what greater honor could there be!

*If You call me to Your court and address me as Your servant,*
*I shall willingly cast away the mastership of both worlds.*

Tafseer of Verses 3 to 4: إِذْ نَادَى رَبَّهُ نِدَاءً خَفِيًّا (٣) قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي وَهَنَ الْعَظْمُ مِنِّي وَاشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا وَلَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعَائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا۔ 

**Zachariah's Prayer for Offspring and His Manner of Supplication:** This is the prayer of Prophet Zachariah, which he offered for the birth of a son. He made this supplication in the Temple (Haikal), likely while in a state of seclusion (I'tikaf), as hinted at later. The text states that he called upon his Lord in secret. This teaches us that making supplications in a private, intimate manner is among the primary etiquettes of prayer and serves as an excellent recommendation for its acceptance. Indeed, such prayers are free from ostentation and show, and it is within them that a servant reveals the true secrets of their heart. For this reason, it is essential to observe this etiquette of supplication, with congregational prayers being the only exception.

**An Effective Prelude:** The true meaning of "wahanal-'adhmu" is: "Let alone my outer body, even my bones within have become hollow." Zachariah established this prelude before presenting his actual request. There is no doubt that he set up such an effective introduction that—if it is not considered bad etiquette to say so—it is a prelude capable of pulling down the mercy of God. Zachariah presented two things as his recommendation: first, his weakness and frailty, and second, his lifelong experience of his Lord's treatment of him. He states, "O my Lord, I have never been disappointed when calling upon You." Consider this: how could a petitioner who has never returned empty-handed from a door be turned away in this state of old age and frailty, when even the marrow of his bones has dried up?

وَإِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوَالِيَ مِنْ وَرَائِي وَكَانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عَاقِرًا فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا (٥) يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَاجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا (٦) ۔ 

**Supplication for a Bearer of Religious Inheritance:** "Mawali" refers to a person's paternal cousins, kinsmen, and relatives by marriage. It appears that Zachariah's relatives were not righteous people, and there was an apprehension that after his death, they would fail to maintain the religious traditions of Zachariah and the family of Jacob, which were the true distinguishing assets of this pure household. He was anxious for a person to arise from this family who could keep the family's mission alive and carry the traditions that constituted the true inheritance of the house of Jacob. Although he himself had entered the final stage of old age and weakness, and his wife was barren—meaning that as far as physical means and circumstances were concerned, there was no expectation of this desire being fulfilled—he was aware of the secret that means and causes are in the hands of God, and God is not a slave to physical means. For this reason, he prayed: "O Lord, though the means are non-existent, everything is within Your power. Grant me, from Your own presence, an heir who can manage my religious inheritance and that of the family of Jacob, and O Lord, make him of pleasing character, free from the weaknesses that have crept into this family."

يَا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيَى لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا۔ 

**Sincere Supplication and its Acceptance:** When a prayer is made at the right time, for the right purpose, with sincere emotion, and in the right words, no distance remains between it and its acceptance. All the veils of physical means are lifted. Though there is darkness and despair on all sides, and circumstances appear entirely unfavorable with no ray of hope in sight, when a servant throws himself at his Lord's door with the hope that "this is the only door for me; whatever I have received has been from here, and whatever I shall receive will be from here," God's mercy ultimately emerges from a corner he could never have imagined. It appears in such a grand manner that the slaves of physical causes are left dumbfounded. Zachariah's prayer was for the right purpose, at the right time, and with sincere emotion; therefore, before the words could fully leave his tongue, the glad tidings of its acceptance were sent down. He was told: "O Zachariah, We give you glad tidings of a son whose name will be Yahya (John)." "Lam naj'al lahu min qablu samiyya." "Samiyy" means an equal, peer, or namesake. It appears later in this Surah in verse 65: "Do you know of any peer to Him?" This was to reassure Zachariah: "Though you are old and your wife is barren, and there is no existing parallel of offspring being born to an old man and a barren woman, My will is to grant you such an unparalleled child."

قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَكَانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عَاقِرًا وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ الْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا 

"'Itiyya" means for something to exceed its limits and go beyond control or manageability. "Wa qad balaghtu minal-kibari 'itiyya" means: "I have reached such an extreme stage of old age that I no longer have full control over my limbs and nerves."

**A Question for Further Reassurance:** Zachariah expressed the hesitation he felt regarding the physical conditions of this glad tiding to gain further peace of mind. He said: "My wife is barren, and I have reached such a stage of old age that my nerves are failing. In such a state, how shall I have a son?"

قَالَ كَذَلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا۔ Contextual clues show that this answer was delivered by an unseen voice (Hatif-e-Ghaib). The omission of the predicate of "kadhalik" serves as evidence of emphasis and certainty. The meaning is: "This is the decision of your Lord; it shall happen exactly like this. For the God Who brought man into existence from absolute non-existence, how can it be difficult to create a child from an old father and a barren mother?"

قَالَ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ لِي آيَةً قَالَ آيتُكَ أَلا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلاثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا۔ 

**Another Request for Peace of Mind:** Zachariah made another request to secure peace of mind. This glad tiding was the manifestation of his deepest heart's desire and was of immense importance to him, which is why he wanted his heart to be fully at ease from every aspect. Zachariah had heard this glad tiding from an unseen voice, so it was possible for him to think that this might be the work of his own imagination, or the echo of his own heart. He requested Allah Almighty to show him a sign so that he could be completely certain that this glad tiding was indeed from his Lord, and that neither his own self nor Satan had any part in it. Similar requests by other prophets are also mentioned in the Qur'an. This serves as evidence that the prophets exercised extreme caution when receiving visions and unseen voices. This does not, God forbid, cast any doubt on their faith. Allah accepted this prayer of Zachariah as well, and designated the sign of this divine glad tiding to be: "You will be able to continuously glorify and praise Allah for three consecutive nights and days, but you will not be able to utter any other word to the people." Obviously, a state coming over a man where he can engage in the remembrance of Allah but cannot utter any other words to people cannot be a satanic state; it can only be a merciful, divine state. Consequently, this state came over Zachariah. When he came out of the sanctuary to his people, he could not speak. He only gestured to them to remain occupied in glorifying and praising Allah.

Some people have taken "alla tukallima" as a prohibition (nehye) rather than a statement of fact. In their view, the verse means that when Zachariah asked for a sign, he was commanded not to speak to anyone for three consecutive nights. Those who have taken this meaning have neither bothered to consider what sign Zachariah had actually requested, nor have they pondered what aspect of a sign could exist in a command to remain silent for three days.

**The Meaning of "Sawiyya":** "Sawiyy" refers to someone free from disease or defect. The meaning is: "Though you will not be able to speak to anyone for three nights and days, this state will not be the result of any illness or defect; rather, while possessing perfect health, it will occur solely by Allah's command as a sign." Just as it was said regarding Moses's white hand: "It will come out white without any disease (min ghayri soo')," here too the word "sawiyya" serves as a safeguard. Its use in the sense of "consecutive" or "continuous" is not common. Later in this Surah, in verse 17, this word appears where it clearly means healthy, well-formed, and of sound stature.

**"Layali" (Nights) Encompasses Both Night and Day:** The word "layali" here encompasses both nights and days. In Surah Al-Imran, the same subject is expressed using the word "ayyam" (days). The term "ayyam" also applies to both night and day. These usages are well-known in the Arabic language.

فَخَرَجَ عَلَى قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَى إِلَيْهِم *أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا۔* "Mihrab" refers to a chamber or antechamber of the temple.

**The Secret within the Directive of Glorification:** It is deduced from this verse that Zachariah was in seclusion in some corner of the temple at the time of this supplication. Surah Al-Imran also informs us that he was standing in prayer when this glad tiding descended upon him. After this, he came out to his people and gestured to them to continuously occupy themselves in glorifying and seeking forgiveness. Within this gesture lay the hidden message that he had been made the trustee of a major secret of divine power, the time for whose manifestation had not yet arrived! The people should wait for it by keeping busy in praising and glorifying God, and see what emerges from the veil of the unseen.

يَا يَحْيَى خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا۔ 

**The Meaning of "The Book" and "Wisdom":** "The Book" clearly refers to the Torah. No separate book was revealed to Prophet Yahya. We have discussed the word "hukm" in detail under Al-Imran: 79. It refers to the capability and power to distinguish between truth and falsehood. This power and capability is the foundation of all knowledge and wisdom. In normal circumstances, this capability develops after reaching maturity and ripens at the age of forty, but it was Allah's special grace upon Yahya that he was granted this precious wealth during his childhood.

**The Meaning of Holding the Book Firmly:** If we reflect here, we find that before this verse, a omitted context is understood: that ultimately, according to Allah's glad tidings, Yahya was born, reached the age of maturity, Allah blessed him with prophethood, and directed him to hold His book firmly. The meaning of holding the book firmly is that Satan and his allies are the eternal enemies of this book, and they will exert all their strength to turn you away from it; therefore, neither fear, greed, nor anything else should frighten or entice you away from it. Consequently, it is known regarding Yahya that he laid down his life for the sake of this Book.

Tafseer of Verses 13 to 14: وَحَنَانًا مِنْ لَدُنَّا وَزَكَاةً وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا (١٣) وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ جَبَّارًا عَصِيًّا۔ 

"Hanan" means love, eagerness, passion, and deep empathy. This word is among the most well-known and commonly used terms, which makes it surprising that some have attributed a statement to Ibn Abbas claiming he did not know its meaning.

**The True Life of the Heart and Soul:** This empathy and love are the primary signs of the life of a human's heart and soul. If these are absent, a human is not a human but a statue of stone. Yahya was granted a very abundant share of this empathy, as is clear from the words "min ladunna" (from Us). To get some idea of his empathy and intense love for God, one may read his sayings in the Gospels. The reality is that the warmth of his words still warms hearts and moves souls today.

**The Concept of "Zakah":** Following "hanan", the word "zakah" appears in his description. "Zakah" means purity and cleanliness. This purity refers to both outer and inner purity, which is a reflection of "hanan" itself. If inner softness is present, no trace of moral or doctrinal pollution remains in either the inner or outer self.

Following "zakah" is the word "taqiyya" (godfearing). The first two qualities relate primarily to a human's inner self. This word praises his outer actions, morals, and character, indicating that he was extremely pious and godfearing. His entire life was one of world-renunciation. He proclaimed repentance with such passion that the deserts and mountains echoed with it. When he spoke in the temple, people's hearts trembled, yet his connection with this world was only to give to it; he took nothing from it. He subsisted on wild honey and locusts. He covered his body with a blanket garment, and the head for which he never built a roof to cover in this world, he gave up for the sake of God's Book, departing from this life.

**Loyalty to Parents:** "Wa barran bi-walidayhi wa lam yakun jabbaran 'asiyya" — This describes his loyalty to his parents. Despite knowing that everything from his birth to his education and upbringing occurred under Allah's direct supervision, and that he was not dependent on his parents for anything, he did not fall into any such misunderstanding. He remained exceptionally loyal, obedient, and respectful to his parents. He was neither arrogant nor disobedient.

وَسَلامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا۔ 

**Blessings and Peace in Every Phase:** This refers to the greetings of peace from the angels. On the day this man of truth entered the world, the heavenly hosts welcomed him with peace and blessings; on the day he died, they received him with a welcome of ease; and on the day he is resurrected, they will greet him with the same welcoming salutation. This is an indication that whatever this world did to him, before God in every phase, his treatment was and will be one of peace and blessings.

Subsequent Subject Matter. Verses 16 to 36: **The Mutual Resemblance Between Yahya and the Messiah:** Next is the mention of Maryam, the birth of Jesus from her womb, and the original teachings of Jesus. Consider the previous mention of Yahya as a prelude to this description. Yahya is both a predecessor and a partner to Jesus in terms of lineage, and he closely resembles him in the nature of his birth. By mentioning both of them together, Allah invites the Christians to open their eyes: why did they create such a massive distinction between two prophets of the same lineage coming one after another, making one of them a god, when the births of both were more or less similar and the teachings and call of both were the same from the cradle to the grave? Recite the following verses in this light.

وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ انْتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِهَا مَكَانًا شَرْقِيًّا۔ 

By "Al-Kitab" is meant the Gospels, in which portions of Maryam's life are described. We have referred to them in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran. For this reason, we will limit our discussion here to clarifying the context of the verses.

**The Eastern Side as the Women's Place of Worship in the Temple:** "Intibadh" means to completely cut oneself off from people and go to one side. Here it means that Maryam retired in seclusion to the eastern side of the temple. It was the eastern side because the portion of the temple designated for women's seclusion and worship was in the east. In maps of Jerusalem from that era, the women's place of worship is shown on the eastern side. The fact that Christians made the east their Qiblah is largely due to this, as they considered the eastern side of the temple to be their special direction.

فَاتَّخَذَتْ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ حِجَابًا فَأَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا۔ 

**Maryam's Trial:** Placing a screen between herself and the people is a sign that she sat in seclusion. During this time, Allah sent His angel to her, who appeared before her in the form of a healthy and well-formed man. It appears that Allah willed to test Maryam by having the angel appear in human form; otherwise, this was not the common manner of angels appearing even to prophets. It is not easy to estimate the impact this event must have had on Maryam, who was as yet unfamiliar with angels and unseen voices, but the character she displayed at this critical moment is exemplary.

قَالَتْ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمَنِ مِنْكَ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَقِيًّا۔ With great dignity, she addressed the appearing figure, saying: "If you have any fear of God, I seek refuge from you in the Most Merciful (Al-Rahman)."

قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لأهَبَ لَكِ غُلامًا زَكِيًّا۔ The angel said: "I am only a messenger from the very Lord of Mercy in Whose name you seek refuge from me, sent to grant you a pure son."

قَالَتْ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا۔ 

"Baghiyy" refers to an unchaste or loose woman.

Maryam was startled upon hearing this statement from the angel. She said: "How shall I have a boy, when no man has touched me, nor have I been unchaste?"

**The Groundless Narrative of Marriage in Luke:** This statement of Maryam makes it absolutely clear that the narrative in Luke claiming she was married to a man named Joseph is entirely groundless. If she had been married, this news would have been a highly blessed glad tiding for her, and she would have thanked Allah instead of expressing anxiety and astonishment in these words. In our view, this narrative in Luke is a mischief introduced by the Jews, as they are vehement opponents of the miraculous birth of Jesus. Those among us who have copied this narrative without due thought have unwittingly served the purpose of the Jews. It must be remembered regarding Maryam that, in accordance with her mother's vow, she was dedicated to the service of the temple, so the question of her marriage does not arise. The servants of the temple were completely free from all domestic ties. But let us temporarily set aside this fact and consider: if Maryam were married to someone, what would be the context for the words she uttered? These words are fitting only from the tongue of a chaste virgin; they are not fitting from a married woman. Furthermore, the words with which her family members reproached her, which appear later, would also become entirely out of place in that case.

قَالَ كَذَلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُ آيَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَقْضِيًّا 

The conjunct of "wa linaj'alahu" (and that We may make him) is omitted here based on contextual clues. If we expand it, the complete statement would be: "Your Lord says: We shall do this so that We may make him a messenger to the Children of Israel, and that he may be a sign and a mercy from Us for the people."

**Prophet Jesus is a Sign:** That Jesus is a sign is mentioned in various places in the Qur'an; see Surah Al-Anbiya: 91 and Al-Mu'minun: 50. The miraculous birth of Jesus is a major sign of the Resurrection. The primary doubt the ignorant have regarding the Resurrection is how people can be recreated without physical causes. The existence of Jesus is the answer to this doubt: that everything comes into being through Allah's command "Kun" (Be). Jesus came into existence through this very word. Consequently, on this basis, he is referred to as "Kalimatullah" (the Word of God) in both the Gospels and the Qur'an.

فَحَمَلَتْهُ فَانْتَبَذَتْ بِهِ مَكَانًا قَصِيًّا۔ 

**The Meaning of "A Distant Place":** "Qasiyy" means distant. There is no explicit clarification here as to which distant place is meant, but the Gospels indicate that she went to Bethlehem. Obviously, upon realizing her pregnancy, she must have been in a state of intense mental anxiety and anguish. In such circumstances, a person's familiar environment becomes distressing, and they desire to isolate themselves and find another refuge, hoping to find peace there. This move of Maryam was of that very nature.

فَأَجَاءَهَا الْمَخَاضُ إِلَى جِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ قَالَتْ يَا لَيْتَنِي مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَذَا وَكُنْتُ نَسْيًا مَنْسِيًّا۔ "Jidh'" means trunk, and the definite article on "an-nakhlah" (the date-palm) indicates that this tree was already known to her. It is possible that in her state of mental anxiety, she received an unseen indication through a vision that when the time of delivery came, she should go to a specific date-palm, where her essential needs would be provided. Consequently, when she felt the labor pains, she went beneath the designated tree.

**A Reflection of Maryam's Inner Feelings:** *She said, "Oh, I wish I had died before this and was in complete oblivion!"* Imagine the state of a chaste virgin from a noble household when she saw what she perceived to be her disgrace unfolding before her eyes! This phrase is a precise expression of her inner feelings. She said, "I wish I had died before this disgrace, and not just died, but been completely erased from people's memories."

فَنَادَاهَا مِنْ تَحْتِهَا أَلا تَحْزَنِي قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا۔ Contextual clues show that the subject of "nada" is the angel, and the pronoun in "min tahtiha" refers to the date-palm. "Sariyy" means a small stream.

**Glad Tidings to Maryam After Passing the Trial:** Maryam's grief had reached the extreme expressed in the previous verse, yet despite this, she did not complain to anyone other than her Lord, nor did she take any wrong step out of despair. Allah willed only to test her. Having succeeded in this trial, the mercy of the Creator suddenly emerged smiling from an unexpected quarter, and Allah displayed for His believing, obedient servant such wonders as have never been shown for anyone else in human history. The angel called out from beneath the tree: "Do not grieve anymore. The days of sorrow are over. Below is a stream, and here is this date-palm which will provide fresh dates for you. Eat, drink, and let your eyes be cooled by the sight of your child."

وَهُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ تُسَاقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَبًا جَنِيًّا۔ "Jidh'" refers to the trunk of the tree. Maryam's shaking of the date-palm trunk was merely a physical cause for the manifestation of divine mercy; otherwise, it is obvious that her physical strength was not such that she could shake a date-palm. The style of expression indicates that just as someone might instruct their butler to present breakfast or food, she was told to touch the trunk of this date-palm, and it would present fresh, ripe dates for her.

فَكُلِي وَاشْرَبِي وَقَرِّي عَيْنًا فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ الْبَشَرِ أَحَدًا فَقُولِي إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنْسِيًّا۔ That is: "Eat, drink, and let your eyes be cooled by the sight of the child, and vow a fast to the Most Merciful. If anyone comes and asks anything, tell them: I have vowed a fast, and I cannot speak to any human being today."

**The Fast of Silence:** In the law of the Jews, silence was among the conditions of fasting. Although the verse does not explicitly state that the angel instructed Maryam to fast, this is clear from the context. When the angel instructs her: "If anyone asks, tell them: I am fasting and cannot speak to anyone," it naturally follows that she was instructed to fast. Otherwise, how could the angel instruct her to make an untrue statement?

It might occur to someone here that in the state Maryam was in, a woman is not permitted to fast. The answer to this doubt is that among the Jews, there existed a fast of mere silence, and this fast could be kept in any state. In Jewish scriptures, the expression "be silent" is used in various places to denote remembering God and worshipping Him; see Zachariah 2:13.

**"Qawl" in the Sense of Gesturing:** Another point may strike some people: if this was a fast of silence, why was the word "qooli" (say/tell) used? A word meaning "to gesture" should have been used instead. The answer is that in Arabic, "qala" is also used in the sense of gesturing. Phrases like "qala bi-rumhihi" (he gestured with his spear) and "qala bi-yadihi hakadha" (he gestured with his hand like this) are well-known in Arabic idioms, and the latter idiom has been used in some Hadiths.

Tafseer of Verses 27 to 28: فَأَتَتْ بِهِ قَوْمَهَا تَحْمِلُهُ قَالُوا يَا مَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْئًا فَرِيًّا (٢٧) يَا أُخْتَ هَارُونَ مَا كَانَ أَبُوكِ امْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَمَا كَانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا (٢٨) ۔ 

**Reproach of Maryam by Her Family:** After receiving the guidance from the angel, Maryam returned to her family carrying the newborn in her arms. As soon as they saw her, they fell upon her. Everyone reproached her and appealed to her honor: "How shameful that someone whose father was noble, whose mother was chaste, and who is the sister of a righteous man like Aaron, could commit such an act!"

"Aaron" here does not refer to Prophet Moses's brother Aaron, but to a person of righteous reputation within Maryam's own family. This is also supported by Hadiths.

فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ قَالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمَ مَنْ كَانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا۔ Since Maryam was observing a fast of silence and could not speak, she pointed to the newborn, indicating: "Take the answer to these accusations from him; there is no need for me to say anything."

Obviously, by this time, it must have become clear to Maryam that the newborn had come fully equipped from God to defend himself and her. The people were very annoyed by Maryam's gesture. They said: "We are asking you, how can we speak to a child who is still in the cradle?!"

**The Manifestation of Divine Power for Maryam's Blessing:** When Maryam's trial reached this peak and she proved successful in every phase, the time arrived for Allah to make His declaration of what wonders He displays for a male or female servant who succeeds in His trial.

Tafseer of Verses 30 to 33: قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا (٣٠) وَجَعَلَنِي مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنْتُ وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا (٣١) وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا (٣٢) وَالسَّلامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدْتُ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا (٣٣)

**The Pronouncements of the Messiah in the Cradle:** These are the pronouncements made by the Messiah from the cradle to exonerate himself and his mother. For Maryam to emerge with honor from the trial she had been put into, it was necessary for the child in her arms to testify to her chastity and his own high status, leaving no room for anyone to slander her. We have explained these pronouncements of the Messiah in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran, and will confine ourselves to brief indications here.

**The Meaning of the Word "Ibn" (Son) in Hebrew:** The first thing he said was: "إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ" (Indeed, I am the servant of Allah). That is, no one should fall into the misunderstanding due to my miraculous birth that I am some superhuman entity. I am indeed a servant of Allah. This is the correct translation of his statement "Ibn Allah" (Son of God) provided by the Qur'an. In Hebrew, the word "Ibn" is used for both a servant and a son, with the context determining its meaning. In the presence of Jesus's clear teachings, there was no room for ambiguity in interpreting this word, but Paul made it a source of trial and constructed an entire system of Trinity around it. We have refuted this mythology in the light of the Gospels in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran and Surah Al-Ma'idah.

**The Addition of Wisdom to the Torah:** The second thing he said was that God had blessed him with the Book and prophethood, and wherever he might be, his existence is a source of goodness and blessing. As far as the Law (Shari'ah) is concerned, Jesus followed and called to the Law of the Torah. However, he added "Hikmah" (wisdom) to it. These are the sermons of wisdom that we find in a scattered and incomplete state in the Gospels. These are the gems he scattered from his blessed tongue in every valley, reviving any heart that retained even a spark of life.

**The Entire Religion and Law Rest Upon Prayer and Zakah:** The third thing he said was that he was instructed to observe prayer and Zakah as long as he lived. These two practices constitute the foundation of all religion and divine laws, which is why they are mentioned first in all heavenly dispensations. Their outer forms have varied in different religions, but the spirit of devotion to the Creator and compassion for the creation has been preserved in every form. Prayer connects a person correctly with his Lord, and Zakah establishes his connection with the creation on a proper footing. The preservation of the entire religion depends on these two pillars. If anyone demolishes them, he demolishes the entire religion, regardless of how much he boasts in the name of religion.

**Obedience to the Mother:** The fourth thing he said was that God made him dutiful to his mother, and did not make him arrogant or wretched. That is, although the nature of my birth is unique and my Lord's blessings upon me are special, despite all this, I am the son of a mother and an obedient son. No one should assume that God's special blessings have altered my human and mortal status. At the same time, he made it clear that whoever is not dutiful to his parents is arrogant and wretched. This statement of the Messiah refutes those narratives in the Gospels which suggest that he did not show proper respect to Maryam. It appears that when the accusation of divinity was falsely attached to Jesus, such narratives were inserted into the Gospels to support it.

وَالسَّلامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدْتُ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا۔ 

**Peace and Greetings in Every Phase:** Just as in verse 15, Allah promised peace and greetings to Yahya in every phase of his life and death, here Jesus announces peace and greetings from the angels for himself in every phase of his birth, death, and resurrection. This verse clearly indicates that just as every mortal passes and will pass through the phases of birth, death, and resurrection, so too did Jesus pass and will pass. Drawing assumptions based solely on commentary narratives that contradict this, without any authority in the Qur'an, is contrary to caution.

Tafseer of Verses 34 to 35: ذَلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ (٣٤) مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ مِنْ وَلَدٍ سُبْحَانَهُ إِذَا قَضَى أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ (٣٥)

**A Timely Parenthetical Warning:** These two verses, placed within the pronouncements of the Messiah, are parenthetical statements from Allah. They timely draw the attention of people in general and Christians in particular to the fact that this is the true reality of Jesus, which he clarified with his own tongue.

**The Myth-Making of the Christians:** "Qawl al-haqqi alladhi feehi yamtaroon." "Imtira'" originally means to squeeze a cow's udder thoroughly. From this, the word came to be used for arguing obstinately to make a mountain out of a molehill, and creating various illusions and doubts about a matter. It is stated that the true reality of Jesus is what Allah has described and what Jesus himself made clear, but the Christians turned it into a myth through endless speculation, falling into illusions themselves and leading others astray. We have discussed Christian theology in detail under Al-Ma'idah: 17; a look at it reveals how Paul turned a simple matter into a riddle.

**The Shirk of the Christians:** "Ma kana lillahi an yattakhida miw-waladin subhanahu." This refutes the shirk of the Christians who took Jesus as the son of God. It is stated that it is entirely contrary to God's attributes to take anyone as a son. "Subhanahu" — He is completely pure of all such attributions. "Idha qadha amran fa-innama yaqoolu lahu kun fayakoon." A son is needed by one who is dependent on another to execute his plans. God is not dependent on anyone; when He wills a matter, His will is fulfilled by His word "Kun" (Be). This phrase also implies that Jesus's miraculous birth is no proof of his divinity; it too is a wonder of God's word "Kun". God willed that he be born in this manner, so he was born without a father through the command "Kun".

وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هَذَا صِرَاطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ۔ 

**The True Meaning of "Ab" (Father) in Hebrew:** After the parenthetical statement, the final portion of Jesus's pronouncements is quoted, where he added: "Allah is indeed my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him; this is the straight path that leads to God." The phrase in the Gospels attributed to Jesus: "My Father and your Father" finds its correct translation here. In the Hebrew language, the word "Ab" is used for both a father and a lord. Its correct meaning is determined by its context. Obviously, in the phrase "My Father and your Father," it can only mean Lord. If it were taken in the sense of a literal father, then Jesus would have no special distinction; rather, the entire creation would become the offspring of God.

Subsequent Subject Matter. Verses 37 to 40: In the following verses, the Christians are reproached for their internal differences, and they are warned of a Day when their entire record will be presented before an All-Knowing God. Recite the verses.

فَاخْتَلَفَ الأحْزَابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ مَشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ ۔

**The Sectarianism of the Christians:** That is, the true reality of Jesus is what has been described above, and this is so clear a matter that there was no room for difference or dispute, yet the Christians created differences due to their wretchedness, splitting into many sects. Among them, only the few who followed Simon Peter remained on the truth. These were the ones who believed in the call of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and whom the Qur'an has praised. All others who followed Paul split into various groups. The Catholics and Protestants are the two well-known sects, but there are many other churches among them.

**The Meaning of "Mashhadi Yawmin 'Adheem" (The Witnessing of a Great Day):** In our view, this refers to the testimony that the prophets will give before Allah on the Day of Resurrection regarding their respective nations. The details of this testimony are described in Surah Al-Ma'idah. Note the critical portion of Jesus's testimony: "And [beware of] when Allah will say, 'O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, "Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?"' He will say, 'Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who are Knower of the unseen. I said not to them except what You commanded me—to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness. If You should punish them—indeed they are Your servants; and if You should forgive them—indeed it is You who are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'" (Al-Ma'idah: 116-117)

In the verses under discussion, the Christians are warned of this very "Great Witnessing". Obviously, when Jesus himself gives the testimony described in the verses of Al-Ma'idah, the Christians will not be able to face Jesus or Allah. Nothing will remain for them then except destruction and lamentation.

أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنَا لَكِنِ الظَّالِمُونَ الْيَوْمَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ ۔ "Asmi' bihim wa absir" are forms of wonder in Arabic. The meaning is: Today, when the true reality is recited and explained to them, they neither hear it nor see and understand it. But when they are confronted with that Great Witnessing, their ears will be opened and the veils will fall from their eyes. It will appear then that there is none more seeing or hearing than them, but that will not be a time for hearing and understanding; it will be a time for lamentation. "Dhalimoon" (wrongdoers) here refers to those unfortunate people who wronged their own souls by stumbling in the dark despite possessing eyes and ears.

وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِيَ الأمْرُ وَهُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ وَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ ۔ 

**The Meaning of "The Day of Regret" (Yawm al-Hasrah):** The Day of Regret refers to the day of that Great Witnessing. On that day, everyone's eyes will open, but all doors of repentance, reform, and action will be closed. Those who are currently in heedlessness and do not believe will say in regret: "If only we could return to the world to live a life of faith and righteous deeds!" But their regret will remain mere regret. On that day, all matters will be decided, and everyone will face the consequences of their actions.

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ الأرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا وَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ ۔ That is, on that day, the sole Owner and Inheritor of the earth and everyone on it will be Allah, and everyone will be presented before Him. On that day, no one will be able to help anyone, nor will anyone be able to intercede without Allah's permission.

Subsequent Subject Matter. Verses 41 to 63: Next is a brief reference to the lives, teachings, and calls of Prophets Abraham, Moses, Ishmael, and Idris (peace be upon them). The purpose of this reference, on one hand, is to clarify to the Christians that all the great prophets before Jesus—descended from Adam, Noah, Abraham, and Israel—called to the very message that Allah's Messenger is presenting to you today, but you proved to be unworthy successors who wasted their teachings and fell into innovations and misguidance. On the other hand, it warns the Quraysh that Abraham and Ishmael, to whom they claim a connection and whose religion they claim to inherit, had nothing to do with the myths they worship today in the name of the religion of Abraham. The true religion of Abraham is the one to which the Qur'an calls you, yet you deny it. Recite the following verses in this light.

وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَدِّيقًا نَبِيًّا۔ 

**The Reason for Prioritizing the Mention of Abraham:** Since Prophet Abraham is the accepted leader of both lineages of prophethood—established through Isaac and Ishmael—his story and call are mentioned first so that proof may be established against both the Children of Israel and the Children of Ishmael.

**The Meaning of "Al-Kitab":** Most of our commentators have taken this to mean the Qur'an. Although this has a context, I have taken it to mean the previous scriptures. In my view, this word here and wherever it appears in connection with the mention of the prophets serves as a reference. That is, by referencing the Torah and the Gospel, it is reminded that the call and teachings of all the great prophets have been the same as what the Qur'an presents. Those who reject this Qur'an are, in reality, denying their own prophets and scriptures. If it is taken to mean the Qur'an, the meaning would be: "Recite to the opponents in this Book the stories of their prophets so they may learn a lesson." This is a mercy and blessing for them; if they do not benefit from it, they deprive themselves of Allah's blessing without harming anyone else. In both cases, the application of the word changes slightly, but the ultimate meaning of the text remains similar.

**A Special Aspect of Mentioning Abraham's Truthfulness (Siddiqiyyah):** Here, the aspect of Abraham's truthfulness is specifically highlighted. The reason is that both the People of the Book and the Arab mushrikeen are being reproached: "You claim the inheritance of Abraham and claim to be his offspring, yet you have completely ruined his call and teachings, replacing them with a new religion. Abraham did not break his covenant with God like you; he was a man of truth, uprightness, and complete loyalty." He succeeded in the trials Allah put him through, and as a reward for succeeding in those trials, Allah honored him with leadership over nations. "You can only be worthy of his inheritance and connection if you fulfill with complete uprightness the covenant you made with God through Abraham. Without this, you have no right to a connection with Abraham, nor any share in the leadership Allah promised to Abraham and his offspring." In Surah Al-Baqarah, Abraham's truthfulness and its reward are mentioned as follows: "And [remember] when Abraham was tried by his Lord with commands and he fulfilled them. [Allah] said, 'Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people.' [Abraham] said, 'And of my descendants?' [Allah] said, 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.'" (Al-Baqarah: 124)

By "commands" (kalimat) is meant those trials in which Abraham was tested and in which he succeeded completely. These trials are mentioned in detail in the Qur'an, and we have referred to them in the commentary of Al-Baqarah. It was as a reward for succeeding in these trials that Allah honored him with the title "Siddiq" (truthful) and elevated him to the leadership of nations. At the same time, in response to Abraham's question, Allah made it clear that this position is not tied to lineage but to actions and character. Only those among Abraham's offspring who uphold their father's truthfulness will be worthy of it. Those who wrong their own souls and become champions of shirk and disbelief have no share in it, whether they belong to the Children of Isaac or the Children of Ishmael.

To understand the true force of the verse under discussion in Surah Maryam, it is necessary to keep in mind all these implications of the word "Siddiq"; without this, the true direction of the subsequent discourse cannot be determined.

Tafseer of Verses 42 to 45: Abraham's Speech to His Father: إِذْ قَالَ لأبِيهِ يَا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لا يَسْمَعُ وَلا يُبْصِرُ وَلا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئًا (٤٢) يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جَاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي أَهْدِكَ صِرَاطًا سَوِيًّا (٤٣) يَا أَبَتِ لا تَعْبُدِ الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَنِ عَصِيًّا (٤٤) يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَنْ يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمَنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَانِ وَلِيًّا (٤٥)

This is the speech Prophet Abraham made to his father, Azar, immediately after being blessed with divine revelation. The repetition of "ya abati" (O my father) in this speech is evidence of Abraham's deep concern, compassion, and gentleness. The anxiety and care that a dutiful son should feel regarding his father's misguidance is apparent in every phrase. This also shows that Azar was Abraham's father, not his uncle as the Jews claim and as some of our commentators have accepted without due thought, which the Sabians then used as a source of trial. We have refuted this biblical narrative in its place.

**Some Truths of This Speech:** In this speech of monotheism, Abraham drew his father's attention to several truths in a natural order:

First, he drew attention to the point: what is the sense in taking these stone statues, carved with your own hands, as deities and worshipping them? Taking someone as a deity is not a matter of hobby or recreation; it relates to human's greatest need. Human believes in God and worships Him because He hears their prayers, sees their suffering, and assists them in every difficulty. What is the use of these statues carved by your own hands, which neither hear, see, nor can benefit you in any way, that you bow before them? This is proof of the self-evident falsehood of shirk: that even apart from its inner reality, its outer form testifies that it is open foolishness, entirely incompatible with intellect and nature.

Second, he clarified that in the matter of God, determining whether He has a partner or not, and who that partner is, is not a matter of mere conjecture. Human believes in one God because the testimony of nature, intellect, horizons, and souls exists, and every human of sound nature is compelled to believe in Him. But what compulsion is there to believe in others, unnecessarily loading oneself with partners to God? In this matter, the only reliable thing is "Al-'Ilm" (the Knowledge)—that is, the true knowledge that comes from God through revelation. Abraham invited his father not to follow mere illusion in such an important matter, but to follow him. He was presenting the true knowledge that had come to him from God. This knowledge leads to the path that reaches directly to God—the "straight path", meaning a path that bypasses all intermediaries and leads directly to God. In other words, it is the path of monotheism.

Third, he clarified that Satan's greatest enmity, as is clear from the story of Adam and Iblis, is with this straight path of monotheism. He has sworn to exert all his strength to turn Adam's offspring away from this straight path and plunge them into shirk. Such loyal obedience to an open rebel against the Lord of Mercy is, in reality, worshipping him; and wretched is the human who abandons God to worship Satan.

Fourth, he clarified: "Until now, you had an excuse that God's guidance had not reached you, but now that God's guidance has reached you, no excuse remains. Therefore, I fear that you may face God's punishment and become a companion of Satan, facing the same end destined for Satan and his allies."

قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنْتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ لأرْجُمَنَّكَ وَاهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا۔ 

"Maliyya" is used in the sense of a lifetime or a long period. In our view, the underlying structure is: "wahjurnee hijran maliyya" — meaning: "Get out of my sight, and never show me your face again."

**Azar's Anger:** Upon hearing Abraham's speech, Azar's anger flared up. He said: "O Abraham, are you turning away from my gods? If you do not desist from this, I will stone you. It is better that you get out of my sight and never show me your face again."

It should be kept in mind here that in tribal life, just as masters possessed unlimited power over their slaves, fathers possessed unlimited power over their sons and daughters; they could kill them, stone them, or bury them alive, with no one to stay their hand.

Tafseer of Verses 47 to 48: قَالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِي حَفِيًّا (٤٧) وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَمَا تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَأَدْعُو رَبِّي عَسَى أَلا أَكُونَ بِدُعَاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا (٤٨)۔ 

"Salam" here carries the meaning of a parting salutation. Just as the polite and blessed way to meet is with a greeting of peace, the polite way to part is also with a greeting of peace. "Innahu kana bee hafiyya" — "Hafiyy" refers to someone who keeps close track of someone, takes great care of them, and is exceptionally kind to them.

When Abraham saw his father so enraged, he said: "Very well, if it is your opinion that I should depart, then receive my peace; I am leaving. There is no room left to discuss anything with you, but I will pray to my Lord to forgive you. He has always been exceptionally kind to me; I hope He will accept my prayer."

**Compassion for the Father and Zeal for the Truth:** Despite the father's stony attitude, Abraham's promise to pray for his forgiveness is evidence of his extreme compassion and softness of heart. Other places in the Qur'an clarify that he continued to pray for his father until Allah forbade him from doing so.

But along with this compassion, he also fully discharged the demands of zeal for the truth. He did not allow any compromise or complacency in his call to monotheism out of politeness or fear; rather, as he departed, he stated clearly: "I am distancing myself from you all, and from all those deities you worship besides God." He further clarified: "I do not call upon, nor will I call upon, anyone other than my Lord, and I hope that by calling upon my Lord, I will not be disappointed."

**Soft as Wax, Firm as Stone:** The strength, trust in God, and independence from creation in Abraham's declaration of dissociation is apparent in every word. First, Abraham used the plural form "wa a'tazilukum" (I am distancing myself from you all), which indicates that he dissociated himself not only from Azar but from all his followers, supporters, and family. Along with this, by saying "wa ma tad'oona min doonillah" (and what you call upon besides Allah), he included all their deities with them, casting them aside. He did not stop at stating this from the negative aspect alone, but made it clear from the positive aspect as well, stating: "wa ad'oo Rabbee" (and I call upon my Lord)—I worship only my Lord, and am familiar with no other deity. Finally, he expressed his unwavering trust in his Lord: "I have never been disappointed when calling upon my Lord; I hope that in this trial too, His help and guidance will be with me." On one hand, there is that softness, and on the other hand, this firmness! Indeed, it is this combination of softness and firmness that distinguishes a caller to truth from others. Until a person is as soft as wax and as firm as stone, they cannot render any service to the truth.

فَلَمَّا اعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَكُلا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا۔ 

**Migration and its Blessings:** Abraham's declaration of dissociation was synonymous with migration, and consequently, he migrated after this. Following this migration, Allah blessed him with Isaac, and subsequently with Jacob, and elevated each of them to prophethood. The manner in which Isaac and Jacob are mentioned in the verse indicates that they are among the fruits and blessings of Abraham's migration. A servant who leaves his home, father, uncle, and all relatives for the sake of his Lord is worthy of Allah arranging a blessed assembly for him, so that the world may see how those who strip themselves for God's sake are settled with honor. Consequently, this aspect of God's power was displayed for Abraham. After migration, Allah not only blessed him with offspring, but with such offspring from whom arose two great lineages of prophethood, messengership, and guidance in this world, whose benefit has continued for thousands of years and will continue until the Day of Resurrection. The mention of Jacob alongside Isaac, while he is Abraham's grandson, is to show that the father, son, and grandson were all prophets—this entire house was filled with light! If we also include Joseph, an entire household of prophets was established. Who else besides Abraham received this honor?

**Dispelling a Doubt:** At first glance, a point may strike us: among Abraham's offspring, Isaac is mentioned, who is the first link in the Israelite lineage, but Ishmael is not mentioned, from whom the Ishmaelite lineage runs and within whom the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), was sent—even though the first and greatest blessing of migration, as proven by our mentor in his treatise *Dhabih*, was Ishmael. In our view, the reason for not mentioning Ishmael here is that his mention is coming independently further on. In this Surah, as we have indicated earlier, the primary addressees are the Arab mushrikeen, while the People of the Book are mentioned secondarily. This nature of the Surah required that Ishmael not be mentioned secondarily here, but rather separately with special emphasis, to fully draw the attention of the Arabs.

Keep also in mind that the prelude to the mention of Jesus was established with the mention of Zachariah, to whom Yahya was born under circumstances where Zachariah had reached extreme old age and his wife was barren. Then, after mentioning the birth of Jesus, Abraham was mentioned, and it is known that he too was granted offspring in old age. Consequently, it is mentioned in the Qur'an that when Abraham was given the glad tidings of a child, his wife expressed astonishment in the exact manner that Zachariah did upon receiving the glad tidings of Yahya's birth. By mentioning both these prophets before and after the mention of Jesus, the Qur'an has implicitly provided the guidance that examples of miraculous births existed before Jesus as well. To attribute divinity to someone solely on this basis is sheer foolishness.

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنَا وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا۔ By "Rahmah" (mercy) is meant all those blessings, honors, and favors, both in terms of prophethood and worldly blessings, which were granted to Abraham and his family, the details of which have passed in the commentary of Al-Baqarah.

**The Meaning of "Lisan-e-Sidq-e-'Aliyya" (A High Reputation of Truth):** In "lisan-e-sidq", "lisan" (tongue) means remembrance, mention, and reputation. Within the word "sidq" lies the concept of stability, durability, and strength. This is a genitive construction similar to "qadam-e-sidq" (footing of truth) used in another place. The meaning is that Allah granted great success to their call, and they achieved a durable honor and reputation that has been granted to no one else in the world. The durability of this reputation is such that thousands of years have passed, yet it has not faded. Hundreds and thousands of great prophets and reformers arose from this blessed family, keeping Abraham's mission alive. Finally, from the lineage of Ishmael, the Master of the Universe, Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), was raised upon this very religion of Abraham, granting this greatness eternal duration. All of this is the blessing of Abraham's prayer, referenced in Surah Ash-Shu'ara as: "And grant me a reputation of truth among later generations."

وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ مُوسَى إِنَّهُ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولا نَبِيًّا۔ 

**The Title "Mukhlas" (Chosen) for Prophet Moses:** Following Abraham, within the Israelite branch of his offspring, is the great prophet Moses. Regarding him, it is stated that he was "mukhlas" and a messenger and prophet. His being a messenger and prophet is completely clear, as he was sent as a messenger to Pharaoh and his people, and to the Children of Israel. However, the word "mukhlas" here points to a special, distinguishing attribute of his. "Mukhlas" refers to someone chosen and designated for a specific, special task. In the general sense of the word, all prophets are "mukhlas" because they were chosen for a special divine mission. Consequently, regarding them, it is stated in the Qur'an: "Indeed, We chose them for a distinctive quality: remembrance of the home [of the Hereafter]." (Sad: 46). For this reason, the question arises: what is the special, distinguishing attribute of Moses due to which he was given this title, when it is not used for anyone else in the entire Qur'an? In our view, the answer to this question is that this word refers to the special distinction Moses had of speaking directly with God. This distinguishing attribute of Moses is mentioned in various places in the Qur'an. We have discussed this under the verse of Surah An-Nisa: "And Allah spoke to Moses directly." The subsequent verse also points to this meaning, stating: "wa qarrabnahu najiyya" (and We brought him near, confiding in him). For this high status of nearness, direct speech, and confidential conversation, only Moses was specially designated, and to express this unique characteristic, he was honored with the title "Mukhlas".

وَنَادَيْنَاهُ مِنْ جَانِبِ الطُّورِ الأيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْنَاهُ نَجِيًّا۔ "Ayman" means both "on the right" and "blessed/sacred". In our view, it is used here in the sense of blessed and sacred. Parallels in the Qur'an support this meaning. In Surah Ta-Ha, the same subject is expressed as follows: "And when he came to it, he was called, 'O Moses, Indeed, I am your Lord, so remove your sandals. Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa. And I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed.'" (Ta-Ha: 11-13)

This verse makes it clear that the voice Moses heard came from the direction of the sacred valley of Tuwa. This valley achieved this status of sanctity because Allah honored it by making it the place of His manifestation, and there He spoke to Moses. This sanctity is expressed as "Ayman" in the verse under discussion, and this was a matter worthy of mention. As for the other meaning of the word, pointing out the right side of the mountain does not seem to carry any specific significance. "Wa qarrabnahu najiyya" — "Najiyy" refers to a secret or whisper, and it also refers to the person with whom one converses confidentially and makes a confidant. This is a clarification of the special distinction of Moses mentioned in the previous verse. It is stated: "We called him from the blessed side of the mountain and brought him close for confidential conversation." The speech Allah had with Moses on this occasion is described as a secret and confidential conversation. The reason is that when Allah spoke to other prophets, He always did so through the intermediary of His near and trusted angel, Gabriel; He never spoke directly. This honor was granted only to Moses, with whom He spoke without the intermediary of Gabriel, with no third party between them. However, on this occasion too, despite his eagerness, Moses was not granted the vision of Allah. This is because in this physical world, no jinn or human—not even a mountain—can bear the manifestation of God. There is no mention here of the matters discussed between Allah and Moses on this occasion; the details will appear in Surah Ta-Ha.

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنَا أَخَاهُ هَارُونَ نَبِيًّا۔ 

**Another Special Favor Upon Moses: The Sending of a Supporting Prophet:** Moses was also granted the special favor that, to assist him in discharging the duties of prophethood, Allah appointed his brother Aaron as his minister and helper, and this assistance was not merely voluntary but in the capacity of an appointed prophet. Appointing a prophet as a minister and partner to assist a messenger is a unique distinction which, in the history of the prophets, is known for no one other than Moses. When the responsibility of delivering the message before a tyrant like Pharaoh and reforming a difficult nation like the Children of Israel was placed upon Moses, he was deeply anxious due to this double and massive responsibility, and prayed: "And appoint for me a minister from my family—Aaron, my brother. Increase through him my strength and let him share my task, that we may glorify You much and remember You much." (Ta-Ha: 29-34). Allah accepted Moses's prayer, and this is mentioned in Surah Ta-Ha as a major favor. This favor is referenced here in the verse under discussion. It should be kept in mind that to defame Aaron, the Jews placed the responsibility for some of their major sins upon him, but the Qur'an has presented him as an innocent prophet.

وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَادِقَ الْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولا نَبِيًّا۔ 

**The Mention of Ishmael with Special Care:** Now, the mention is made of Ishmael, the head of the other branch of Abraham's offspring. The Quraysh were of his descent and claimed to follow his religion. The first messenger and prophet in this line was Ishmael, and the final prophet and messenger is our Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The purpose of his mention is to warn the Arab mushrikeen to consider what Ishmael's actions and message were, and how distant they are from him, yet they claim his inheritance and in this vanity oppose the Qur'an—even though it calls to the religion of Abraham and Ishmael.

**Ishmael was "Sadiq al-Wa'd" (True to His Promise):** His special attribute is described as: "إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَادِقَ الْوَعْدِ" (Indeed, he was true to his promise). Obviously, this refers to the promise he made to Abraham regarding his sacrifice. When Abraham consulted him: "I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you, so see what you think," he replied without hesitation: "O my father, do as you are commanded; you will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast." When the time arrived to fulfill this promise, he placed his neck beneath his father's knife without hesitation, and the knife was about to run, but Allah stayed Abraham's hand: "The purpose was the trial, and it has been fulfilled!" Both father and son were granted the certificate of loyalty and uprightness from Allah. Remember that "Sadiq al-Wa'd" consists of only two words, but it is a comprehensive expression of the character of a believer and Muslim. A servant of Allah who is upright in the covenant he makes with his Lord, and is willing to have his neck cut for its sake, has reached the pinnacle of faith and Islam. As for those who were merely exploiting the names of Abraham and Ishmael out of lineage pride, the Qur'an placed this mirror before them so they might observe their own tarnished faces!

وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُ بِالصَّلاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ وَكَانَ عِنڈ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا۔ 

**Ishmael's Special Mission:** The status Ishmael held in faith and Islam was made clear by the single expression "Sadiq al-Wa'd" above. Now, his descendants are reminded that he used to instruct his people to observe prayer and Zakah. Prayer and Zakah, as we have indicated earlier, constitute a comprehensive expression of all the rights of Allah and all the rights of His servants. Upon these two practices rests the foundation of all laws; whoever maintains them maintains the entire religion, and whoever demolishes them demolishes the entire religion.

**A Servant Pleasing to His Lord:** "Wa kana 'inda Rabbihi mardhiyya" is such a comprehensive praise that there remains no room to add a single letter to it. Its true meaning is that he was in the eyes of his Lord precisely as He desires His servant to be. For someone regarding whom the Creator Himself testifies that he met the standard of His pleasure, who could be more complete in devotion?

It should be kept in mind here that in their zeal against Islam, the Jews have completely distorted the history of Abraham and Ishmael. Specifically, they have drawn a veil over Ishmael's life so that no trace of him may be found, but our mentor, Maulana Farahi, in his treatise *Al-Dhibh*, has exposed all these alterations and revived the history of these two great prophets. Those who wish to appreciate the value of these Qur'anic descriptions regarding Abraham and Ishmael should study the Maulana's book with a deep eye.

Tafseer of Verses 56 to 57: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِدْرِيسَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَدِّيقًا نَبِيًّا (٥٦) وَرَفَعْنَاهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا (٥٧)

**Prophet Idris:** Regarding Prophet Idris, I have not been able to find anything in Jewish scriptures or biblical history on the basis of which anything can be said about him with certainty. What ancient and modern commentators have written is based entirely on conjectures and assumptions, making its citation useless. The manner in which the Qur'an mentions him indicates that his mention existed in the scriptures of the People of the Book, and some true and false narratives regarding him were well-known among them. It may be that, as with most prophets' names when adapted into Arabic phonetics, the name of Idris was altered. It must be remembered that the Torah was lost several times and compiled from oral traditions several times. For this reason, differences arose in its manuscripts, and additions and subtractions continued. Our view is that just as the Qur'an has traced many lost or obscured truths of the Torah, of which many examples have passed in this book, it has traced a lost leaf of the history of the prophets by mentioning Idris. His character, like that of other prophets, was an exemplar for all humanity, which is why the Qur'an reminded of him from the correct perspective and revived him in history.

**Qualitative Sharing Between Idris and Ishmael:** In his praise, the exact same word appears as in the praise of Abraham above: "Siddiq" (truthful). The implications of this word have been described. Obviously, Idris achieved this status only after passing through many trials. It is also worthy of attention that Idris is mentioned here alongside Ishmael, and in Surah Al-Anbiya too he is mentioned alongside Ishmael, where both are counted among the steadfast (Sabireen): "And [mention] Ishmael and Idris and Dhul-Kifl; all were of the patient." (Al-Anbiya: 85). Reflecting on both these places reveals a deep qualitative resemblance between these two prophets, which is why they are mentioned together. They had to pass through very severe trials of patience and steadfastness, and as a reward for passing them, they were honored with that high status before Allah referenced by the words: "wa rafa'nahu makanan 'aliyya" (and We raised him to a high position).

Tafseer of Verses 58 to 59: أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْرَائِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَاجْتَبَيْنَا إِذَا تُتْلَى عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتُ الرَّحْمَنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا (٥٨) فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا (٥٩)

**The Common Mission of All Prophets:** Now, pointing to all these mentioned prophets, it is stated: these great prophets are the choice flowers of the offspring of Adam, Noah, Abraham, and Israel. Allah's special favor was upon them, and they are among those whom Allah guided to His religion and chose for the status of prophethood and messengership. The common attribute of all of them was that when the verses of God were recited to them, they would fall down in prostration, weeping. Then it is stated: after them, there arose in their offspring unworthy successors who wasted prayer and followed lusts, so they will soon face the consequences of this misguidance.

**A Warning to the Unworthy Successors:** Indeed, this was the primary purpose for which the stories of the mentioned prophets were narrated. The addressees of the Qur'an at that time, whether the Arab mushrikeen or the People of the Book, all claimed to follow one or another of these prophets, and they accepted that these were the sources of God's guidance among the offspring of Adam, Noah, Abraham, and Israel. The Qur'an has referred to the call and character of all of them, lamenting the state of their unworthy successors who claimed descent from Abraham and Jacob and claimed to follow Moses, Jesus, and Ishmael, yet had ruined the entire religion taught by these great prophets.

That it is said regarding these prophets: "when the verses of Allah were recited to them, they fell down in prostration, weeping" is a reproach to their self-proclaimed followers, whose state was the exact opposite: when the verses of the Qur'an were recited to them, they denied them with extreme arrogance and mocked them. In "fa-khalafa min ba'dihim khalfun", the word "khalf" is used in the sense of an unworthy successor. We have explained elsewhere the difference between "khalaf" and "khalf": "khalf" with a sukoon on the lam is used not in a good sense, but for bad descendants. The reason these people were unworthy successors is that connection and inheritance with prophets are not achieved on the basis of family and lineage, but on the basis of carrying the guidance they brought. Their state was that they ruined prayer and became slaves of lusts. Obviously, by wasting prayer, they lost the very core of the religion. Prayer is the very practice which, if performed correctly, reminds the servant of the covenant he made with his Lord. If this is wasted, it is certain that human will fall into the hands of Satan. The treatment the Jews, Christians, and mushrikeen meted out to this fundamental command has passed in detail in Surah Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-An'am; repeating it here would lead to length.

**A Point Worthy of Attention:** It is worthy of attention here that it is not said: "they wasted prayer and Zakah," but rather: "they wasted prayer and followed lusts," even though both prayer and Zakah are mentioned in the teachings of all prophets above. Thus, one might expect Zakah to be mentioned here as well. This change in style points to a specific truth: that what prevents human from Zakah and spending in the way of Allah is, in reality, this following of lusts. A person who occupies himself with satisfying his lusts becomes so enslaved by them that no room remains for him to spend in the way of God. This mentions the primary obstacle to Zakah and spending in place of mentioning the practices themselves, showing that this obstacle dominated them. In "fasawfa yalqawna ghayya", "ghayya" refers to the consequence of action. They will face their misguidance—meaning they will face the end of their misguidance. This points to the truth that what will confront them in the Hereafter will be the harvest of what they themselves sowed. In this matter, no injustice will be done to them.

Tafseer of Verses 60 to 63: إِلا مَنْ تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَأُولَئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْئًا (٦٠) جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدَ الرَّحْمَنُ عِبَادَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ إِنَّهُ كَانَ وَعْدُهُ مَأْتِيًّا (٦١) لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا إِلا سَلامًا وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا (٦٢) تِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا مَنْ كَانَ تَقِيًّا (٦٣)

**What Benefits in the Hereafter:** That is, lineage and status will prove of no benefit; however, those who turn back from their errors and reform their actions will enter Paradise, and they should rest assured that they will not be wronged in the least; rather, they will receive the full reward of every good deed. As a reward for their actions, they will receive eternal gardens in the world of the unseen. This is the promise of the Lord of Mercy to His righteous servants, and His promise must be fulfilled. "Ma'tiyya" means that all rightful servants of God will certainly reach this promised destination of God. No one should consider this a mere promise for tomorrow and fall into doubt regarding it.

**Peace and Blessings on All Sides:** "La yasma'oona feeha laghwan illa salama." That is, all the noise and storm of opposition currently raised against the truth and the people of truth will vanish there. No vain talk will strike the ears. On all sides, there will be exchanges of peace, blessings, and congratulations. The dwellers of Paradise will congratulate one another on their success and victory, and the angels will welcome them on their triumph with greetings of peace.

**The True Provision for the Dwellers of Paradise:** "Wa lahum rizquhum feeha bukratan wa 'ashiyya." Morning and evening clearly refer to the morning and evening of Paradise, the true nature of which will be known only in Paradise. The true provision for the believers is the vision of God, His peace, His message, and His honor. This will be granted to them continuously there. If length were not apprehended, we would cite some Hadiths here in which this subject is clarified beautifully. The word "Rizq" is also used in other places in the Qur'an for divine lights, blessings, and spiritual provisions—for instance, in Al-Imran: 37—and this expression exists in other heavenly scriptures as well.

**A Warning to those Claiming Hereditary Rights to Paradise:** "Tilkal-jannatu allati noorithu min 'ibadina man kana taqiyya." It is stated: We will make those among Our servants who are godfearing and respect His boundaries the inheritors of this Paradise. Every claimant and vain desirer will not become worthy of it. This phrase is a reproach to those who had ruined the entire religion of God, yet in their vanity considered themselves the hereditary owners of Paradise.

Subsequent Subject Matter. Verses 64 to 98: **Conclusion of the Surah:** Next are the concluding verses of the Surah. First, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is exhorted to patience and steadfastness against opponents through the words of Gabriel. Then, the opponents are reproached for their denial of the Resurrection. Specifically, a heavy blow is struck at their mindset where they consider the worldly superiority they possess over the believers to be proof of their being on the truth, claiming that even if the Resurrection occurs, they will achieve a high status there as well due to their presumed partners and intercessors. Finally, the Prophet is instructed to be indifferent to their demands for punishment and to continue warning and giving glad tidings through the Qur'an—as it is fully equipped with all requirements of establishing proof in every phase. Those who have fear of God will benefit from it. As for those who are obstinate and argumentative, inform them of their end. If they persist in their obstinacy, they will face its consequences; you are free from responsibility for them. Recite the verses in this light.

Tafseer of Verses 64 to 65: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ اِلَّا بِاَمْرِ رَبِّكَ ۚ لَهٗ مَا بَيْنَ اَيْدِيْــنَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِـيًّا۔ رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالأرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهِ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُ سَمِيًّا 

**An Exhortation from Gabriel in the Middle of the Discourse:** This speech is from Gabriel, in which he exhorts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to patience and steadfastness, and clarifies his own status and that of other angels: that they are not authorized to do anything of their own accord, and their task is only to execute the commands of Allah. This exhortation to patience and this clarification have a specific context. At this stage of the conflict between truth and falsehood, the sole support for the Prophet against his opponents was the support of divine revelation. Through this heavenly aid, he received strength and guidance in difficulties, and through it, the new objections and fitnahs raised by the opponents were refuted. For this reason, the Prophet naturally waited with intense anxiety for Gabriel, who was the medium for bringing divine revelation. This anxiety of the Prophet was an essential demand of the circumstances. A warrior who is on the front lines against a massive army of the enemy always waits for guidance from the center so that no wrong step is taken. But every action of Allah occurs under His wisdom and benefit, which only He knows. For this reason, the Prophet is exhorted to patience and waiting in various places in the Qur'an: "Remain firm on your stance, do not hasten; the guidance and help that is needed will be sent down by Allah at its proper time." The same subject is expressed in Surah Ta-Ha as: "And do not hasten with the Qur'an before its revelation is completed to you, and say, 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge.'" (Ta-Ha: 114). This subject appears in other places in the Qur'an as well, but here it is spoken from the tongue of Gabriel, which adds greatly to its eloquence. We wish to point out some of its aspects.

The first thing worthy of attention is that this is a sort of apology from Gabriel to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "If we do not descend quickly with revelation to satisfy your eagerness and waiting, this is not due to any shortcoming on our part, but our descent is entirely under the command of Allah. Until He commands, we can neither descend nor bring any revelation to you. We have no part in this matter; we are only commanded to execute His orders."

**The True Status of Gabriel and the Angels:** The second thing worthy of attention is that this clarifies the status of Gabriel and the entire assembly of angels: that everything before them, behind them, and between them is under Allah's supervision and complete control. None has the power to exceed their domain by a hair's breadth or take any step of their own will. Allah is not forgetful of anything, such that anyone could exploit His forgetfulness to exercise free will in any matter and remain safe from His grasp. The clarification of this status of the angels was necessary to strike at the foolishness of the Arab mushrikeen who worshipped the angels, hoping that if the Resurrection occurred, these "daughters of God" would speak to their father and have them forgiven. These foolish people were informed through this statement of Gabriel that when such is the helplessness of Gabriel—the chief of all angels—before the Lord of Majesty, what of others!

**Reassurance to the Prophet:** Within "wa ma kana Rabbuka nasiyya" (and your Lord is never forgetful), another aspect comes to mind. This is reassurance to the Prophet: "If there is ever a delay in divine revelation, rest assured that this delay is based on some wisdom and benefit, for your Lord does not forget anything; He is aware of all your circumstances and difficulties and remembers every prayer and supplication. There is no fear of Him forgetting anything."

رَبُّ السَّمٰوٰتِ وَالْاَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهٖ ۭ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهٗ سَمِيًّا۔ The word "Samiyy" passed in verse 7. It means peer or parallel.

This verse can either be part of Gabriel's statement, or a completion of Gabriel's statement by Allah by way of insertion. Examples of such insertion are many in the Holy Qur'an; one example passed in Surah Al-Kahf. Whichever of these two forms is adopted, the difference will be only in the speaker, with no difference in the meaning of the speech; and this difference of speaker is also merely apparent, because whatever Gabriel says, he says only by the command of Allah. Allah commanded him to clarify his excuse regarding the revelation, so he did.

My preference is for the first view: that Gabriel exhorted the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to patience and waiting: "Your matter is not with just anyone, but with the Lord of all the heavens and the earth and everything between them; so worship Him and remain firm on His worship with complete stability and steadfastness." Contextual clues show that the word "Ibadah" (worship) here includes both worship and obedience in its wide sense. We clarified this truth in the commentary of Surah Al-Fatihah. In verse 44, this word is also used in the sense of obedience. Within "istabir" lies greater force than "sabara". Remember the rule of Arabic grammar that an increase in letters indicates an increase in meaning; and if "sabr" or "istibar" is followed by "li", it also encompasses the meaning of waiting. We have tried to reveal these implications in the translation, but these delicate aspects of the Arabic language are difficult to capture in translation. This subject will also appear in Surah Ta-Ha, where we will throw further light on it, if Allah wills.
